intro: JavaScript provides a highly dynamic, garbage-collected runtime optimized for user interactions and web web services. Moving to C++ upgrades the software stack to compiled high-performance execution, zero-overhead abstractions, and precise object lifecycle control. This transition is common when building graphics engines, high-frequency trading platforms, or resource-constrained applications.

diffs.to: Manual allocation or automated RAII/smart pointer tracking.

diffs.from: JIT compilation inside web browsers or Node.js containers.
diffs.to: Direct AOT compiled machine binary output.

diffs.from: Promises and native microtask queue loops.
diffs.to: Multi-threading via std::thread, futures, or async worker channels.

examples.title: Class Blueprint Declarations
examples.description: Building custom data types containing encapsulated properties and methods.

examples.title: Vector Sequence Manipulation
examples.description: Adding elements dynamically into tracked arrays.

tips.title: Utilize Smart Pointers
tips.body: Avoid bare "new" or "delete" operators by managing dynamic allocations with std::unique_ptr and std::shared_ptr.

tips.title: Leverage RAII Design
tips.body: Bind resource allocation and release to object lifecycles so resources clear automatically when leaving scope.

tips.title: Pass Parameters by Reference
tips.body: Use const references (const std::string& arg) to avoid expensive data copying overhead during function execution.

tips.title: Leverage the STL Container Suite
tips.body: Replace dynamic JavaScript arrays with specialized standard library containers like std::vector or std::map.
